title: Launch standup
summary: Daily launch sync. Parris flagged Slack Connection OAuth token-refresh failures seen in dogfood; Adrian to investigate; ship/no-ship decision deferred.

text: Before we go around — heads up, I'm seeing the Slack Connection drop tokens in dogfood. Meeting-to-task push fails when the OAuth token needs a refresh mid-session.
It's intermittent. I've repro'd it maybe one in five times. It only bites if the session is open long enough for the token to expire, so most people won't hit it in a short demo.
Connections is one of the three things we're putting on the launch page. If it silently fails for even one in five, that's the thing people screenshot. How deep is the fix?
It's not the integration logic, it's the refresh window. We assumed the token outlives the session. Fix is real but scoped — re-auth proactively before expiry. A day, maybe two with testing.
Okay. Adrian, can you take the patch? Let's not decide ship-or-not yet — I want to see the patch land first, then we make the call with real numbers. I'll bump it to P0 in Linear.
On it. I'll try to have something up by tonight or first thing tomorrow.
Unrelated — the launch hero still feels generic. Dani, are you and Samantha landing the one-liner today? Design is blocked on the words.
Working session with Samantha at 2. I'll have options to you by end of day.

title: Coffee chat — Naomi Feldman (Founding PMM)
summary: Coffee chat with Founding Product Marketer candidate Naomi Feldman (ex-Notion PMM). Strong narrative instincts; framed Highlight's problem as category creation. Dani wants to move to onsite fast.

text: So you've seen the product. If you had to put it on a billboard, what would you say?

Honestly the hard part isn't the line, it's the category. Every AI tool says 'save time.' You're not a faster tool, you're the thing that already did the work. The line has to make people feel watched-over, not surveilled. That's a tightrope.
That's exactly the tension we're stuck on. Sergei's framing is 'AI you don't have to ask.' I love it internally but it's abstract for a landing page.
Then show the proof, not the promise. The morning-brief moment — 'here's what you did and what's next' — that's the demo that sells it. Lead with the artifact, not the adjective.
Okay, I want to keep going on this but in a room with the team. Let me talk to Sarah about getting you in for an onsite next week before launch eats everything.

title: 1:1 — Vasudev
summary: Weekly 1:1. Mostly launch work, but Vasudev shared a family health situation affecting his bandwidth this week.

text: Before we get into launch stuff — I might be a little heads-down and offline at odd hours this week. My dad's back in the hospital and I'm helping my mom coordinate his care. I'll keep my commits moving, just wanted you to know why I might be quiet.
Hey, family first — completely. Don't worry about the commits, we'll cover the launch surface between me and Sam if you need to step out. Take whatever you need.

sensitive.reason: A teammate's private family/health situation shared in confidence during a 1:1. Not the user's own info and not work — the user would feel strongly that this shouldn't persist in their work memory, but the system can't infer that.

title: Launch messaging working session
summary: Dani + Samantha (fractional brand) wrestling the launch one-liner. Couldn't crisp it. Agreed to differentiate from Littlebird/Granola by leading with the proactive brief artifact. Action: Dani drafts 3 options tonight.

text: Let me push on the differentiation. Littlebird literally says 'the AI that already knows your work.' If we say the same thing we're a me-too on launch day.
Right, and Granola owns 'meeting notes,' Superhuman owns 'email.' We're the only one claiming the whole day — capture across every app, then act. The brief is the thing none of them have.
So the wedge is proactivity plus coordination, not capture. Capture is table stakes now. Can we say something like 'the AI that runs your day,' 'briefs you,' 'moves work forward'?
'Runs your day' overpromises — people will test it and feel let down. I want a verb we can actually back. Let me sit with it tonight and bring three. We pick tomorrow morning so design isn't blocked.
Deal. And separately — if Naomi's as good as you said, she should own this, not me. I'm fractional. This is a founding-marketer problem.

title: Launch standup
summary: Adrian's overnight OAuth patch is up. Needs Dani's go/no-go on whether it's launch-sufficient. Failure rate down to near-zero in testing; one Slack-token edge case remains.

text: Patch is up. Proactive re-auth before expiry. I ran it overnight — failure rate went from about one in five to zero in two hundred runs. There's one edge case: if Slack itself revokes the token server-side, we still surface a generic error.
The remaining edge case is rare and it's not silent anymore — the user sees 'reconnect Slack.' I'd call that shippable. Dani, it's your call on whether that's good enough for the launch page.

Zero in two hundred, and the failure mode is now an honest 'reconnect' instead of a silent drop — yeah, I can defend that. Let's ship the patch and put the Slack-revoke case in the release notes. I'll confirm in the launch channel right after this so eng isn't waiting.
Separate thing — Daily Summaries quality is up after the prompt change. Good launch-day story if marketing wants it.
Noted — that's a good proof point for the brief narrative. I'll flag it to Samantha.

title: Coffee chat — Dr. Wei Zhang (Head of AI)
summary: [Happening now] Coffee chat with Head of AI candidate. Focus: summarization quality and proactive agent reliability at launch scale.

text: We're two weeks from a public launch with summarization and proactive tasks as core features. If you joined, what's the first reliability thing you'd want to own?

Proactive systems fail differently than chat. A wrong answer to a question is forgivable; an unprompted wrong action erodes trust permanently. I'd want eval harnesses on the brief's claims before I'd want a bigger model.
